Indus Waters Treaty

# Indus Waters Treaty

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

May 26, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

After the massacre at Pehalgam in April and the subsequent stand off, one of the acts of Indian Govt has been to suspend the Indus Waters Treaty with Pakistan, that has been in existence since 1960. It is important to note that this Treaty was never suspended even during height of conflict with Pakistan during 1965, 1971 or Kargil, nor even after 26/11, when all contacts were cut off.

**So why is the Treaty significant and how does it affect Pakistan?**

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!icrQ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F762e17dd-4f39-41ee-8a44-8897b59affca_1008x740.jpeg)

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!icrQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F762e17dd-4f39-41ee-8a44-8897b59affca_1008x740.jpeg "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For starters we would need to understand the Indus River Basin, that has 6 major rivers, subdivided into 2 categories, the Eastern Rivers of Ravi, Beas and Sutlej over which India has control, having a total mean annual flow of 41 billion cubic metres. And the Western Rivers of Indus, Chenab, Jhelum over which Pakistan has control, having a total mean annual flow of 99 billion cubic metres. So basically India has 30% control over the rivers, while Pakistan has around 70%.

Also as per the treaty India can use the Western river waters for limited irrigation use and unlimited non cosumption uses like power generation, navigation.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!F-Z2!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5ab7a6ed-c298-461f-b644-d8d4de66d50f_800x864.png)

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!F-Z2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5ab7a6ed-c298-461f-b644-d8d4de66d50f_800x864.png "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**So what are Pakistan’s fears here?**

Pakistan controls the downstream rivers, and has the concern that India being an upstream country could cause floods or droughts artificially. Basically upstream here is the source from where the river waters come, hills, mountains, while downstream is where the water enters the plains, and enters the sea. The source of all the rivers in the Indus River Basin is in India, that means it can just turn the tap off.

**History**

When India became independent in 1947, there was a conflict over the sharing of the Indus River Waters. Around 1948, East Punjab shut off the water towards West Punjab via the Upper Bari Doab Canal at Madhopur, as well as Dipalpur Canal at Ferozepur. Only when Pakistan agreed to attend an Inter-Dominion conference to negotiate an agreement, was the flow resumed.

India’s action forced Pakistan, to take up the matter diplomatically, as the two headed close to war.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!DDM-!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff56389ac-fdd2-401c-8e74-831653df15e3_250x195.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!DDM-!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff56389ac-fdd2-401c-8e74-831653df15e3_250x195.jpeg)

It was then David E. Lilienthal, ex chairman of Tennessee Valley Authority, who came up with a World Bank aided proposal, by which the 2 countries could come to an agreement on water sharing, and that ultimately led to Indus Waters Treaty signed on September 19, 1960.

As per the treaty India not only agreed to water sharing, but also agreed to a 10 year transition period, during which it would supply water to the network of canals in Pakistan, until it was in a position to fully utilize it. Also as per Article 5.1, India agreed to contribute around 62 million Pounds towards construction of new head-works and canal system in Pakistan’s Punjab province. This was honored by India even during the 1965 War between the two.

A Permanent Indus Commission was also created comprising representatives from both countries to oversee and ensure the implementation and management of the provisions in the Treaty. Any construction of dams and projects would be subject to notification and common approval.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9DYs!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5c32ec61-0360-4936-8b8a-95e6bd19ff77_1920x1080.jpeg)

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9DYs!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5c32ec61-0360-4936-8b8a-95e6bd19ff77_1920x1080.jpeg "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Salal Dam in Reasi, Jammu was constructed in such a way. The Tulbul Project a navigation lock cum control structure has been pending construction for decades due to no clearance, even after discussions.

**So what is the effect now of keeping it in suspension mean?**

Contrary to what has been made out, India really does not have the storage capacity to prevent river water flowing into Pakistan. What it certainly does mean is that India now can optimize it’s usage of the Western rivers. Also India now does not need to inform Pakistan when it will stop and release the water. Even though it can do so only for 2-3 days, that would be enough to cause floods and drought at a smaller level.

Also India is building the Palkul Dam with a larger storage capacity, that is expected to be operational by 2026 on Chenab River at Kishtwar. Apart from this hydroelectric projects at Kishanganga and Ratle are also being fast tracked, to use it as a strategic weapon. With the treaty in abeyance, Pakistan’s complaints to international forums, can be safely ignored.

Another concern for Pakistan is the 850 MW Ratle Hydro Electric Project in Jammu, especially after the Chenab river was diverted through tunnels at Drabshalla in Kishtwar district.

As of now India holds all the aces and it’s Pakistan at grave danger here. There is more to explore here, which will be doing in forthcoming posts.
